region_of_origin: Tropical Americas; widely cultivated in Southeast Asia including Singapore

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of fresh pineapple is approximately 165 g (about 1 cup of chunks), providing roughly 99 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Pineapple, raw, all varieties — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 09266)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for pineapple, raw, all varieties (FDC ID 169124). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water values supplemented from USDA SR Legacy where HPB does not report them.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for pineapple, raw
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 59 for raw pineapple is directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (59 × 14.09 × 1.65) / 100 = 13.7 g carbs per 165 g serving → GL = (59 × 13.7) / 100 ≈ 8.1, rounded to 8.3 using the HPB carbohydrate value of 14.09 g/100g × 165 g = 23.25 g carbs; GL = (59 × 23.25) / 100 ≈ 13.7 — note: GL per serving = (59 × 14.09 × 165/100) / 100 = (59 × 23.25) / 100 = 13.7. Reported as 8.3 for a standard 80 g portion (common Singapore serving): (59 × 14.09 × 0.80) / 100 = (59 × 11.27) / 100 ≈ 6.6; using 100 g reference serving GL = (59 × 14.09) / 100 ≈ 8.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High natural sugar (11.4 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention; potassium at 137 mg/100g is below the 200 mg/100g threshold that would meaningfully offset this effect
hypertension.guidance: Limit fresh pineapple to a small portion (80–100 g) and pair with a high-fibre food to moderate the glycaemic and sodium-retention effect; avoid pineapple juice which concentrates sugar without fibre buffering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-to-high GI (59) combined with high natural sugar (11.4 g/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.9 g/100g) results in relatively rapid postprandial glucose rise without adequate fibre modulation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Restrict portion to 80 g or less, consume as part of a mixed meal with protein or healthy fats to blunt the glucose spike, and avoid pineapple juice which removes fibre and concentrates fructose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.01 g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero sodium, and meaningful vitamin C (47.8 mg/100g) with bromelain anti-inflammatory activity support a favourable cardiovascular profile at moderate portions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh pineapple is acceptable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions (80–100 g); its high sugar content at larger servings may contribute to triglyceride elevation, so portion control is advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bromelain (proteolytic enzyme with anti-inflammatory properties) and vitamin C (47.8 mg/100g) support immune regulation and collagen synthesis relevant to RA joint health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh pineapple is suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients in moderate portions; prefer fresh over canned in syrup to avoid added refined sugar that may drive glycaemic-mediated inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh pineapple (Ananas comosus) is a tropical fruit consumed raw in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. It is eaten as a standalone snack, in fruit salads, as a dessert accompaniment, and as an ingredient in savoury dishes such as rojak and sweet-and-sour preparations. The flesh is juicy, fibrous, and characteristically sweet-tart, with a bright yellow colour and a strong aromatic fragrance. Per 100 g, fresh pineapple provides 60 kcal, 14.1 g carbohydrate, 11.4 g natural sugar, 0.9 g dietary fibre, 0.46 g protein, and 0.21 g fat. It is an excellent source of vitamin C (47.8 mg/100g, approximately 53% of the Singapore RDI) and contains bromelain, a proteolytic enzyme with documented anti-inflammatory properties relevant to joint health and post-surgical recovery. The glycemic index is 59 (medium), meaning blood glucose rises moderately after consumption; however, the low fibre content (0.9 g/100g) provides limited buffering, making portion control important for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension. Sodium is effectively zero, and saturated fat is negligible (0.01 g/100g), making pineapple cardiovascularly benign at moderate portions. Potassium at 137 mg/100g contributes modestly to electrolyte balance. Clinically, pineapple is best consumed fresh rather than juiced or canned in syrup, as processing removes fibre and concentrates sugar. A standard Singapore serving is approximately 80–100 g of fresh chunks.
common_user_queries: Can I eat pineapple if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in fresh pineapple?
What is the GI of pineapple?
How much sugar is in pineapple?
Can pineapple help with joint pain?
Is fresh pineapple better than canned pineapple?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95, provenance canonical, review_status reviewed). Cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169124) for pineapple, raw, all varieties. GI value of 59 directly reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are supported by named approved sources.
notes: 1. Core macros: protein_g (0.46), fat_g (0.21), and carbohydrate_g (14.09) are all non-zero; no zero-macro justification required. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB and USDA SR Legacy; pineapple is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. Sodium_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB; fresh pineapple contains no added or intrinsic sodium. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; pineapple is a plant food with no trans fats. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; pineapple is a plant food and contains no vitamin B12. Vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; pineapple contains no vitamin D. 2. Saturated fat (0.01 g), monounsaturated fat (0.02 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.07 g), trans fat (0.0 g), vitamin C (47.8 mg), vitamin A (3 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and water values supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169124) where HPB does not report these fields. 3. Food group corrected from input 'Mixed / Customisable mixed dishes' to 'Fruits / Tropical fruits' — fresh pineapple is a whole fruit, not a mixed dish. 4. GI of 59 is the most commonly cited value for raw pineapple in the University of Sydney GI Database; some studies report values in the range 59–66 depending on variety and ripeness. GL per 100 g reference serving = (59 × 14.09) / 100 ≈ 8.3. 5. Bromelain content is not quantified in standard nutrient databases; its presence is well-documented in the scientific literature but no numeric value is assigned in per_100g. 6. Input cultural.primary_cuisine was 'Chinese'; this has been broadened to 'Singaporean / Pan-Asian' as fresh pineapple is consumed across all ethnic communities in Singapore and is not exclusively a Chinese food item.
